The class to which I have the honour to belong wonders whether the deprivation of a citizen's liberty on the basis of allegations of remarks and actions is not a return to those epochs of painful memory when saturnalias afflicted the country. You will deign to put an end to this doubt by restoring my liberty. I say it again, I address myself to your authority, trusting in your justice. But if, against all expectation, you were to reject my just claim, Monsieur le gouverneur, I should have to lament being unable to feel the salutary effects of
your protective authority, and it would be even more painful for me to carry my grievances 2,000 leagues from this colony.
I am, etc.
VILMARD.
BOURBON.
The colonial council of the island of Bourbon at its last session adopted a resolution by which it contests the government of the metropole's right to emancipate blacks taken in the slave trade prior to the law of 4 March 1831. However, Article 10 of the said law is worded as follows:
Blacks recognized as slave-trade blacks shall be declared free. An authentic act of their liberation shall be drawn up and transcribed in a special register deposited at the court registry. A formal copy shall be delivered to them without charge.
FOREIGN COLONIES.
ANTIGUA.
The much-debated question of the immediate emancipation of slaves or of time-limited apprenticeship for self-redemption has been entirely resolved for the colony of Antigua in the first and most liberal as well as most rational sense. More than two hundred barrels of sugar have been shipped from this island to England; this sugar is of a quality so superior to that previously produced by slave labour that 62 shillings per quintal were offered for part of it.
Since their emancipation the most certain accounts arrive from all sides of the admirable conduct of the blacks of Antigua. The missionaries, travellers, and press of this colony all bear the same testimony to their orderly habits, their tireless industry, their decency, their good conduct, and the attention they bring to their religious duties on Sundays.
The planter of this island increasingly recognizes that free labour
is cheaper for him than slave labour; and that by consenting to the immediate and absolute emancipation of the blacks, he has not only acted in their interest, but also increased his own revenues.
JAMAICA.
A thorough inquiry has taken place on the question of whether the apprenticeship of emancipated blacks was practicable or not. A great number of planters were heard on this point; their individual depositions offer great contradictions; but in summarizing them all in his report to the King of England, the governor-general of Jamaica announces that: The blacks are perfectly quiet; that he has had no insurrectional act to reproach them with, and that their conduct belies the testimony of the planters as to their turbulence; that on the contrary they work on the plantations the number of hours fixed by law, that they have manifested their intention to continue doing so, but that they will not work at any price during the time left to them. The governor adds, that it appears evident to him that the blacks are perfectly capable of distinguishing their friends from their enemies; those who took care of them in times that are no more, from those who have regarded them only as beasts of burden; those who have resolved to make the apprenticeship as light as possible, from those who wish to make it as intolerable as slavery itself.
This report is from November 1834.
The governor, in proroguing the legislative assembly on 28 January, declared that according to the reports he received from all sides there were the strongest reasons to believe that tranquillity would not be disturbed anywhere, and that the precautionary measures which his duty had commanded him to adopt had been carried out without the least danger. He added that the blacks had returned to the sense of their duties and that they were everywhere disposed to work.
TRINIDAD.
A letter communicated to us from a French merchant established at Port of Spain and written in November last contains the following passage: The prospects for the new harvest
are excellent, the apprentices are very assiduous in their duties and the colony enjoys perfect tranquillity.
CAPE OF GOOD HOPE.
On 1 December 1834, the day the bill came into force in this part of the British possessions, the abolition of slavery and the beginning of apprenticeship were celebrated at a public banquet by the principal inhabitants of Cape Town. The newly emancipated slaves drew up on that same day a most remarkable address in which they manifest a truly admirable spirit of order and wisdom.
The free men of this colony, it is there said, take the liberty of addressing their humble thanks to H.M. the King of England and to all the honoured members of parliament, not forgetting their good governor, and in a word all those who have taken an interest in the work of the emancipation of the slaves. If we lack the expressions to render the sentiments of gratitude we feel, we are not wanting at least in those to offer to the Father of all beings our fervent prayer that He may shed His blessings upon our benefactors; we also pray this great God to enlighten our minds so that we may seek His word and walk in His ways.
VARIOUS NEWS.
One reads in the True Sun: All the information reaching us from our colonies continues to speak of the good effect of the emancipation of the blacks, and everywhere this act is being carried out with the greatest ease and the greatest calm. The blacks submit joyfully to the apprenticeship which must precede their emancipation.
We read in the excellent quarterly collection the AbolitionnistAbolitionist: The latest news from the colonies agree in representing them in a most tranquil state.
The colonist planters and their agents, both in the colonies and in the metropole, are making the greatest efforts to achieve their purpose; but, says the Abolitionist, they may cry themselves hoarse; put themselves in open hostility with the executive power;
announce a deficit in revenue; predict the ruin of the colonies; they may besiege the doors of the colonial offices; all of this will be in vain. Nevertheless we urge the friends of humanity to be on their guard, not to divide their forces, and to be ready at the first signal to support the great cause of black liberty. The question of apprenticeship will no doubt be submitted to the forthcoming parliament, and it would be possible that the machinations of the colonial party might render necessary a pronounced demonstration of public opinion against their project of corrupting the best principles of the abolition act and prolonging slavery under new forms and specious pretexts.
While the partisans of slavery are straining to mislead public opinion about the situation of the English colonies freed by the emancipation bill; while they paint these colonies to us as plunged into anarchy, the King of England, in his speech at the opening of parliament, expresses himself thus:
My Lords and Gentlemen,
The reports received from the governors of our colonies, as well as the measures adopted in execution of the abolition of slavery, will be communicated to you. It is with lively satisfaction that I have seen the colonial legislatures generally concur in the execution of this important measure, and despite the difficulties inseparable from such an act, I have no reason to modify my ardent hope for a favourable outcome.
At Salem (United States of America), there was an exhibition of artistic and tasteful objects, made by ladies and sold for the benefit of the fund of the Society for the abolition of slavery. The proceeds amounted to three hundred dollars (1,600 francs). Several articles were remarkable both for the choice of subject and for the mottoes attached to them. There were sugar loaves which were sold with the announcement that they were not made by slaves; (Not made by slaves.) — It is said that the deputation of the aristocracy of the colonies has been completed at the ministry of the navy, by the arrival in France of M. Sully-Brunet, one of the delegates of the privileged colonists of Bourbon. This aristocratic deputation is thus composed: Martinique: MM. the barons de Cools Desnoyer and Charles Dupin. Guadeloupe: MM. Mauguin and Jabrun.
Bourbon: MM. Sully-Brunet and Conil.
French GuianeGuiana: M. Favard.
The king's commissioner to the Republic of Haiti, M. Dupetit-Thouars, left Port-au-Prince on 20 January last and arrived at Brest on 11 February. M. Dupetit-Thouars carries a letter of credit on London for 4,848,904 francs 60 centimes, for the balance of the principal of the debt owed to the French treasury. He is also furnished with all the information that the French government desired about the state of Haiti and the intentions of the government of the republic.
M. Lepelletier Duclary, counsellor at the royal court of Martinique, has been appointed president of the said court in replacement of M. Perrinelle-Dumay. M. Le Royer Dubisson, deputy judge at Fort-Royal, has been appointed king's attorney at Saint-Pierre, in replacement of M. Aubert-Armand. M. Transnon, auditor counsellor in GuianeGuiana, has been appointed to the same functions in Martinique in replacement of M. Olivier.
M. Gibelin, counsellor at the royal court of GuianeGuiana, has been appointed president of the said court in replacement of M. Persezol, whose resignation was forced. M. Dejean, king's attorney at the court of first instance of Cayenne, has been appointed counsellor at the royal court in replacement of M. Gibelin. M. Dalican, auditor counsellor at the royal court, has been appointed king's attorney in replacement of M. Dejean. M. Pujo, auditor judge at Saint-Pierre, Martinique, has been appointed auditor counsellor in GuianeGuiana, in replacement of M. Transnon. M. Cheveux, auditor judge at Basse-Terre, Guadeloupe, has been appointed auditor counsellor in GuianeGuiana, in replacement of M. Dalican.
M. Ruffi de Pontevès, former counsellor at the royal court of GuianeGuiana, has been appointed counsellor at the royal court of Pondicherry, in replacement of M. Saint-Paul.
BIBLIOGRAPHY.
LETTER TO THE MINISTER OF FINANCE on his opinion in the question of sugars, by M. the baron de Cools, pamphlet in quarto. M. the baron de Cools, delegate of the colonists of Martinique, has just
addressed to M. Humann a letter containing some observations on a passage of the speech delivered by him, at the time of the presentation to the chamber of the budget bill for 1836, relating to colonial sugars. It was not difficult for him to demonstrate how great was the minister's error when he affirmed that a reduction of duty on this commodity would have no other result than to diminish the treasury's receipts for the sole profit of the colonists; without any advantage following for metropolitan consumers; without the use of it extending to the lower classes, as a result of a fall in prices which he declares will not come about. Assertions as erroneous, arguments as miserable as those employed by the minister, made their refutation easy; and so it has not cost great efforts to the one who undertook it.
With regard to M. the minister of finance, the delegate of the colonists of Martinique also rightfully claims for his constituents a parity of position, a protection equal to that accorded to the inhabitants of the metropole; Shackled in the bonds of the most exclusive and the narrowest monopoly, colonial producers can demand, as the continental producers heard at the inquiry have done, the absolute prohibition of foreign products similar to theirs. (1) These demands, these claims are perfectly just; but only, we repeat, with respect to M. the minister, we do not see very well on what basis M. Humann, the co-exploiter of whites, could base a refusal to M. de Cools, the representative of the exploiters of blacks. They are both parties interested in the system of monopoly and privileges, their interests are the same, and they could not prosper without mutually lending each other aid and support.
Although we do not have a very perfect knowledge of the common burdens and dangers, borne equally by French citizens and the inhabitants of the colonies, which give the latter, always according to M. de Cools, the right to invoke titles of birth and origin; although our conviction that no power in Europe would wish to take charge of our colonies does not permit us to set a very high value on the fidelity displayed by their inhabitants, in seeking nowhere but under the aegis
(1) Extract from M. Cools's letter.
of France the guarantee of their properties and industries — a guarantee we would call encouragement to the barbarous and ruinous exploitation of a class of men and of the soil they cultivate — whatever may be our scant respect for the claims which, like this one, are made only in the name of an aristocratic system of favours and protection; we shall willingly join the delegate of Martinique, to demand with him the reduction to 25 francs of the duty on sugars, a reduction long proposed by all enlightened organs of public opinion.
But if we give him entirely the benefit of the doubt with regard to the minister when he demands that the French colonists not be placed outside the national family according to the whim or convenience of the day, we cannot dispense with making a similar demand for the most unfortunate and greatest part of the inhabitants of our colonies. He could not without inconsistency, without himself furnishing arms to combat it, claim to retain according to the whim of the colonists an entire people, a race of men, outside the great social family, outside of humanity.
If M. de Cools knew the true interests of those he has undertaken to represent, he would not limit himself to seeking in a lowering of tariffs the means of improving their position, of extricating them from the financial distress into which they have fallen; he would know that the question of wages is the most important and that it is resolved by free labour; he would know further that in the Spanish colonies, where work is done by slaves concurrently with free workers, production is cheaper than in the French colonies; and that in India, where work is done entirely by free workers, products are obtained at a lower price than anywhere else.
If by some impossibility M. the delegate of Martinique were to obtain from the minister the reduction of duty he requests, this might well be a momentary palliative, a one-day obstacle placed in the way of the ruin of our colonies; but this measure would be powerless to destroy the causes that must bring about this ruin, which can only be avoided by placing oneself in the working conditions of the countries that produce cheaply: the emancipation of the slaves, free labour, and the right to sell one's products and to buy those one needs, wherever this can be done with the greatest advantage for all parties.
NOTE ON THE PROSPECTUS OF THE SOCIETY FOR THE ABOLITION OF SLAVERY, by M. Foignet, pamphlet in octavo.
Here comes M. Foignet, former delegate of the colonists of Guadeloupe, who having heard it said that the Society for the abolition of slavery was ready to hear all observations submitted to it on the question of the emancipation of the blacks, has not wished to let pass this opportunity to manifest his goodwill towards the colonial status quo. Consequently, M. Foignet read, within this society, a most curious note, upon my word, which he had printed no doubt for the edification of his former constituents of Guadeloupe. This pamphlet, which is nothing other than a vain dissertation in which the author seeks to reconcile what is irreconcilable — slavery and liberty, right and usurpation — is ultimately nothing but a summary of the sophisms and errors with whose aid the colonists defend their detestable domination, not without covering it with the most beautiful pretexts of humanity; for, you see, these gentlemen only wish to keep the blacks as slaves in the blacks' own interest. The heart bleeds, these good colonists say, when they come to think of what would become of the unfortunate blacks if they were abandoned to the dangers and the inevitable misfortunes of the illusory liberty we demand for them, we other restless spirits and ill-intentioned logicians. M. Foignet makes this very clear in his pamphlet. He prefers for the blacks, not without a great deal of philanthropy, the assured salt cod of slavery to the uncertain dishes of a perilous liberty. M. Foignet is not at all, on the article of liberty, of the same opinion as the Haitians, who have long since adopted the old motto of the Poles: Better a stormy liberty than a peaceful slavery, and who are not doing too badly by it, so it is said. It is known that masters who call themselves Christians have until now carefully maintained among their slaves, also supposed to be Christians, the most disgusting promiscuity and immorality in the relations between the sexes. This gross monstrosity shocked M. Foignet himself; he felt that thought should be given to constituting the family among the blacks; but nothing is more strange than his ideas on this subject. One must
see this in the pamphlet itself: it is truly curious; I say curious, because the good intention of M. Foignet is evident, however absurd and monstrous his system may be.
But we shall push the examination of this pamphlet no further, as it is of no value in the great debate now pending before public opinion. M. Foignet, however, seems to us to have made, here and there in his pamphlet, concessions of principle to the spirit of the age, which will displease the gentlemen colonists of the old school. This is not to say that M. Foignet, with his perpetual prevarications, can please the friends of emancipation any more — those who want it complete and immediate, and not recognized as necessary only in the course of time. No, certainly not: M. Foignet will appear to them one of those men who have lost their footing in the reality of things, and struggle in the void, playing roughly in colonial affairs the same role as the third party plays in the affairs of the metropole. In fact, his pamphlet will satisfy no one; and we shall willingly compare him to the hare of which Homer speaks, who having started running between two armies about to fall upon each other, drew laughter from both camps.
POSTSCRIPT.
M. le duc de Broglie, president of the Society for the abolition of slavery, has been appointed minister of foreign affairs in replacement of M. de Rigny, and president of the council of ministers in replacement of M. le maréchal Mortier.
M. de Rigny has been appointed minister, with a seat on the council, and is charged with the interim portfolio of war.
ERRATUM.
Our first sheet had just been printed when we noticed a serious error committed on page 12 following the name of the honourable M. Gatine; read: BARRISTER AT THE COURT OF CASSATION, instead of: Barrister at the royal court.
